title: Area-contracting maps between rectangles
why_relevant: Guth's thesis is the original source for the sharp up-to-constants estimates for k-dilation of degree-one maps between rectangles. It is the closest match to the stated problem and likely contains the rectangle-specific slicing, tightening, and relative isoperimetric arguments needed here.

title: The width-volume inequality
why_relevant: This paper proves lower bounds for k-dilation of degree-one maps between Euclidean domains and estimates such bounds for rectangles. Its width/sweepout machinery is directly relevant to turning Dil_2(f) ≤ 1 and degree one into side-length inequalities.

title: Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles
why_relevant: Although stated for k-expanding embeddings, this paper contains Guth's rectangle-specific isoperimetric profile estimates, complexes of cycles, and tightening construction. These are exactly the tools needed to prove anisotropic inequalities involving products of side lengths such as S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.

title: Minimax problems related to cup powers and Steenrod squares
why_relevant: This paper develops minimax lower bounds for families of cycles using cohomology operations and explicitly explains how such bounds give lower bounds for k-dilation of degree-one maps. Its appendix discusses degree-one maps between rectangles and the relevant cycle-space framework.

title: Directional isoperimetric inequalities and rational homotopy invariants
why_relevant: This paper proves anisotropic, direction-dependent isoperimetric inequalities and applies them to k-dilation lower bounds. The directional nature of the estimates is highly relevant to the side-length-sensitive rectangle inequality in the problem.

title: Isoperimetric inequalities and rational homotopy invariants
why_relevant: This companion paper estimates isoperimetric constants for ellipsoids and uses them to bound k-dilation of degree-nonzero maps. Its methods for converting filling inequalities and linking/Hopf-type invariants into dilation lower bounds are plausible tools for the 4-dimensional rectangle problem.

title: Contraction of areas vs. topology of mappings
why_relevant: This paper studies when small k-dilation is obstructed by topology, especially for area-contraction. It supplies a modern toolkit connecting Dil_2 bounds, Steenrod-square-type obstructions, and quantitative geometric measure theory.

title: Homotopically non-trivial maps with small k-dilation
why_relevant: This paper gives key constructions showing how small k-dilation can coexist with nontrivial topology in some dimensions. Even though it is mainly constructive, it helps identify which features of the degree-one rectangle problem must be used to rule out rank-collapse/Kaufman-type behavior.

title: Filling Riemannian manifolds
why_relevant: Gromov's paper is a foundational source for filling radius, Federer-Fleming-type isoperimetric inequalities, sweepouts, and systolic/filling arguments. Guth's width-volume and rectangle dilation estimates build directly on this circle of ideas.

title: Isoperimetry of waists and concentration of maps
why_relevant: This paper develops waist and sweepout lower bounds for fibers/cycles of maps. Such estimates are central to proving that a degree-one, area-contracting map must have sufficiently large slices or cycle families in the target rectangle.

title: The homotopy groups of the integral cycle groups
why_relevant: Almgren's paper is the foundational source for the topology of spaces of cycles and the discrete complexes-of-cycles formalism later used by Gromov and Guth. It is relevant for rigorously encoding the sweepouts induced by a degree-one map of pairs.

title: A short proof of Gromov's filling inequality
why_relevant: This paper gives an efficient proof of Gromov's filling inequality for Lipschitz cycles. Filling inequalities are a core ingredient in the tightening and isoperimetric-profile arguments used in rectangle k-dilation problems.

title: On Gromov's waist of the sphere theorem
why_relevant: This paper provides a detailed proof of Gromov's waist theorem. Its rigorous treatment of waist/sweepout estimates can be useful if the rectangle problem is approached by embedding or doubling rectangles and applying quantitative waist arguments.
